[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

(The image? I've finished the sequence of my published books; while I decide what I want to do next, I have some memes to share.)

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Strong Emotions during DA Course
comments.content:
So I'm nearing the end of the Dolmen Arch course, meditating through the final triads. And a couple days ago I got a massive surge of emotion that that lasted for a couple days.

There were some triggers, but they weren't beyond what I usually encounter, and the emotion felt kind of uncharacteristically strong for me. It was ferocious anger one night followed by a sad, sulky mood for two days. Many of the sensations were surprisingly physical: I felt fatigued and listless. My decision-making was off. You have said anger is a secondary emotion, and I suppose what the dominant feeling was one of sorrow or grief, without a clear cause.

Is this a known phenomenon in DA work or similar? If so, what is it? Some possibilities I came up with in meditation are:
1) The personality trying to reassert itself
2) Previously unacknowledged sorrow or grief that comes up during the DA work
3) As part of acknowledging the personality, one builds a greater sensitivity to its moods. Then the next stage is working to balance with it.

I have to admit some failure at using the DA techniques to offset it. I suppose part of me felt a need to just let it run its course, and observe it. I'm back to my normal self today, though.

Still not sure what I've learned from it, other than I am capable of more emotion than I knew. Or rather, capable of that level of sorrow with an unclear cause.

replies.title: Re: Strong Emotions during DA Course
replies.content: Sometimes such things happen in any form of serious esoteric training; it's usually a matter of unresolved emotions from the past releasing themselves. At the same time, I've heard from a lot of people who are dealing with odd emotional outbursts just now, so it may well be collective: the Saturn-Neptune conjunction that perfects on the 20th of this month may be involved.

replies.title: Re: Strong Emotions during DA Course
replies.content: Many thanks for the reassurance. And also for the astrology info. That's good to know. I've also had experiences where I felt emotions that didn't seem like my own in group settings -- especially angry sensations that came out of left field with no identifiable cause -- so the collective energy could be at play. Once I'm done with Dolmen Arch, I should probably have a closer look at astrology!

replies.title: Re: Strong Emotions during DA Course
replies.content:
Data point

There have been some issues in my household, but we are also dealing with grief from a parent who passed away this past autumn (in law for me), and a very close family friend who passed away this past January. I've known that person 22 years, but my partner has known him for 46 years. It all got ramped up this past weekend. It felt like the coming conjunction is a part of it... but I also had dreams about healing and magic yesterday morning before the heavy emotions came along in my partner.

Wishing everyone who will have them peace and blessings.

Mauve Oscillating Lobster Flaneur

replies.title: Re: Strong Emotions during DA Course
replies.content: A couple of other things to consider. It may have been emotions related to a past life, so unless the memory comes back you may never know what it was about. Or it could be something just drifted past in the spirit world and affected you, and it was never really your emotions to begin with.

comments.title: Not cut out for magic?
comments.content:
Can there be such a thing as not being cut out for the occult or ceremonial magic? I realize asking such a question looks silly typed out. Still, it’s a genuine one…
I have been performing neophyte ritual practice for eight months now which has been pretty smooth actually. No abrupt life changes, no sudden psychic overwhelm, I have not experienced anything paranormal or supernatural in that I’ve not heard voices or seen half-human, half-goat creatures or been attacked by demons.
I know this probably sounds a little naive but does that mean I’m doing something wrong? Not going hard enough?
I guess I’m revealing my assumption here that once I start doing the rituals I would attract attention from astral entities, as that’s what I’ve heard from people who started the LBRP and middle pillar ritual.
But does that necessarily say anything about me as an occultist? Does that make me inept or inadequate? Does that mean perhaps I am not very talented at this due to lack of psychic perception? Or perhaps my senses are dull, like a “squib” in Harry Potter terms (I apologize for the reference lol).
Are there any such indicators that are actually valid?

replies.title: Re: Not cut out for magic?
replies.content:
Yes, there are people who are not cut out for ceremonial magic, but the things you've noted don't suggest that you're one of them. The first stages of magical practice are always slow going, because you have to develop the capacity to perceive things that you've been taught all your life not to perceive.

When I was first doing this work -- and it was the same practices you're doing now, the LBRP and Middle Pillar exercise -- it took more than a year before I got the first clear sense that I wasn't just wasting my time. No, that wasn't half-goat critters materializing out of thin air, or anything else you'd find in tacky Hollywood pseudomagic; it was a slight but noticeable sense of clearing, as though a half-seen fog had gone away, when I finished the banishing ritual.

That is to say, relax and keep going. What you're experiencing is normal. Over time, you'll start to notice faint, subtle changes, and then bit by bit they'll become less faint and less subtle -- until eventually they rock your world.

replies.title: Re: Not cut out for magic?
replies.content: Thanks so much John for that encouragement. It was an unexpected burst of hope and optimism (and relief)!

replies.title: Re: Not cut out for magic?
replies.content: I can second this. If you're "not cut out" for occult work there won't be any mystery about it. Jean duBuis would say that an interest in doing it and a willingness to do it even if nothing seems to be happening is an indication that you have passed the "Initiation of the Nadir" and are beginning the climb back up. In any event, not noticing anything isn't at all a sign nothing is happening. Some of the BIG changes are remarkably subtle. For instance, working with subtle energies in the body won't lead you to glow in the dark or look like one of those ridiculous black velvet Saint Germaine paintings you sometimes see. But it WILL lead to your not getting sick as often, or as sick when you do; ad to having generally more energy and, after awhile, energy people like. It won't be for decades that you notice that, even though you're aging, you're not aging like other people. You don't look young, exactly, but you don't look aged either, but more like you've been... refined, carved. That's just from subtle energy work. Everything else is like that as well. There are results, but just not noticeable until, cumulatively, they pop over your threshold of awareness and you realize you are a different person.

replies.title: Re: Not cut out for magic?
replies.content: Thanks for that Gandalf - I’ll add it onto the list of things to look forward to in the coming years of doing this work.

replies.title: Re: Not cut out for magic?
replies.content: I am a bit jealous about the no sudden psychic overwhelm. It took the best of me and two to three years of serious commitment to stabilize my senses. Even though I never had anything close to something resembling a mental breakdown or anything, it can be disorienting. Hearing and seeing things is fun, but if it is sudden it has its dangers. In my relatively short experience in this path, the ones that are more likely to succeed in the long run are the ones that build and train the senses up. To me the fact that you've stuck with it for 8 months in the fave of what you mention says a lot of good things about you as an occultist.

comments.title: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
comments.content:
Dear JMG, I'm the one who mentioned the hoodoo jar of salt and bent nails in the most recent Covid Forum.

For those who missed it:
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/358770.html?thread=59185778#cmt59185778

In answer to another commenter who asked how this works, you wrote:

"Permit me to explain. ;-)

"The jar consists of a glass jar filled with coarse salt with several small nails buried in it. The nails should be iron or steel -- white metal, not yellow metal -- and they should be bent in the middle, which you can do with two pairs of pliers. Unless you live in very damp conditions, it's best if the lid stays off.

"As for what it does, recall that the etheric plane, the plane of life force, is metaphorically "next to" the material plane, and so these two planes influence each other easily. In the modern world, because next to nobody practices even basic etheric hygiene, most of our environments are squalid and filthy on the etheric level, full of decaying etheric patterns. There's also the far from minor point that many kinds of evil magic work on the denser levels of the etheric plane and produce nasty patterns of their own.

"That's where the jar comes in. Sharp pointed iron or steel causes dense etheric patterns to collapse and discharge the life force in them. Salt crystals absorb unwanted etheric energy. The jar works like a gentle etheric vacuum cleaner, scooping up etheric dust and grime and storing it in the salt. You'll know it's time to change the salt when it gets discolored; you'll know it's time to replace the nails when they rust. The more squalid your etheric environment, the faster this will happen. "

#   #   #   #   #   #   #   #

My questions are:

Which size jar would be ideal? I used a jam jar, left the top open, and tucked it on a shelf behind some picture frames. It seems to be working well, however: Does its effectiveness change if the jar is set behind something, in a drawer, set down low or placed up high?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content:
1) Any size convenient to you.

2) No. As long as it's in the room, it'll have its usual effect. I put one in a closed drawer in a bedside nightstand when I need the additional help clearing away etheric nastiness, and it works just fine.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content:
Between the two of you that answers most of the questions I was going to ask! Two fastidious points of detail. Does the jar have to be glass (will plastic do?) and is a single nail adequate?
Thank you

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content: Glass is much better than plastic, since plastic emits gases that can muck up the etheric properties of the salt. A single nail will do, but several are better.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content:
Hang on, plastic emits gases? Just looking around my desk, I'm counting at least thirty objects (yes, I know it's a mess) made largely of plastic. Am I poisoning myself by sitting here?

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content: Yes, most plastics emit gases and vapors, a lot right after they're manufactured, tapering off over time. They also drop little bits of themselves, which is what all that talk about "microplastics" is discussing. Now you know one of the reasons why certain chronic illnesses are so much more common than they were a century ago.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content:
So that's the disgusting smell new plastic objects have. I was aware of microplastics but figured I'd avoid the worst if I don't eat or drink from plastic. If I'm breathing the stuff in anyway, that's presumably not actually doing much to help me. Thanks.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content: Bingo. What you're smelling is a mix of solvents and polymerizers, which gradually outgas from the plastic. None of them are good for you.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content:
I would expect that the plastic has to be subject to moving contact with surfaces or strong currents for it to shed microplastic particles, and it has to either have moving contact with surfaces or be vulnerable to and exposed to UV or moisture for it to generate the particles in the first place.

Different plastics offgas different things, and some might be more harmless than others, but I don't know the chemistry or the biology. Search results claim that polyethylene and polypropylene are better in this connection. I've heard there are some plastics that don't turn into horrible witch's-brews of random chemical combinations when incompletely burnt, and maybe those would be the ones that offgas safer things in their just-produced state.

It's hard to guess how to go from speculating about chemistry and materials science in this context to speculating about salt nail jars. It feels a bit like the difference between being an industrialist trying to predict the effects of weather on the composition of input river water as part of anticipating process input materials costs, and being the same industrialist trying to predict the effects of politics on tariffs as part of anticipating process input materials costs. ... it feels like I should have a short low-drama way of explaining here why I expect all predictions involving subtle bodies to require taking into account "political" effects, but I don't. https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/359082.html?thread=59245738#cmt59245738 touches on it though.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content: Since few of us have advanced degrees in organic chemistry, however, and glass jars are readily available, it's far more useful in practical terms to simply say, "use a glass jar."

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content: At least it coheres with alchemical practice in gathering rainwater.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content: Which is another data point suggesting that a glass jar is a good idea.

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Jar of Salt and Bent Nails
replies.content:
Thank you JMG for answering a question I’ve been asking people I thought would know the answer but were clueless.
My water in SoCal is not very good for drinking so I refill gallons at a place that purifies and remineralizes water (which is quite good). However, I have a few glass containers but it’s more practical to use the chemical free? plastic ones they sell… mine are now quite old so I inquired if old ones at least had less gas off than new ones but no one knew… so thanks!

Jill C

Mather's translation of the Abranelin working isn't complete. Other, more complete manuscripts of it have been found since Mathers' day, and edited and/or translated in recent decades by Jürg von Inns and then by Georg Dehn.

A ceremonial magician named William Bloom undertook the entire operation and published an account of his experience, under the title The Sacred Magician: A Ceremonial Diary, twice: in 1976 (under the pseudonym Georges Chevalier) and then in 1992 (under his own name). Each edition has details that he deliberately omits from the other. I'd advise a careful reading of both editions before undertaking the working: he got results, and they changed him permanently, perhaps not for the better--Bloom himself seems not to be quite sure.

replies.title: Re: Meditation and Divination Questions
replies.content:
1) That kind of stuckness is quite common. Staring at the theme works for some people but not for most. It can help to have a set of questions in your mind that you can use to goose the theme into activity. Try memorizing these seven concepts:

Being, Becoming, Source, Substance, Power, Purpose, Value

And these seven questions:

Whether? What? How? Who? Where? When? Why?

Practice combining them until you can rattle off all 49 combinations without a hitch. (You can vary the wording as needed. Thus the first seven are "whether it is, what it is, how it is, whose it is, where it is, when it is, why it is," and the next seven are "whether it becomes, what it becomes, how it becomes, whose it becomes, where it becomes, when it becomes, why it becomes." Proceed through the rest the same way. Then, when you get stuck on the theme, start generating questions using the combinations. "Whether it is" -- does the thing actually exist, and how do you know one way or the other? "What it is" -- how exactly would you define it? And so on. You can get a lot of meditative mileage out of even the most recalcitrant theme by that method.

2) Yes. You're concentrating life force in your body. It's generally good for you, but if it becomes too hot to be comfortable, imagine the soles of your feet "opening up" and drain the heat off into the earth. (Ladies in menopause take note: this also helps with hot flashes.)

3) Well, when somebody says, "Oh, he's just impossible!" do you have a sense of what they're saying?

A note on Saturn returns - in traditional astrology the Return is not a point in time, but the entire time that Saturn is in its home sign - so it lasts around 2-1/2 years. In a traditional sense your Saturn return started this past weekend. The Return is at its most intense or culmination of sorts when it is exact by degree, but you are in it. This should become more evident when Saturn gets within ten degrees orb of its home position.

And, this time around you can celebrate the start of your Return with the Saturn/Neptune conjunction this week. It is possible that the themes of this week persist for your through the period.

You can also note the aspects transiting Saturn makes to other planets in your chart. I've been through two of them now, and in them there was a parallel series of events in the same order over a period of around a year and a half.

replies.title: Re: Houses in Mundane Astrology
replies.content:
Orbs in general are much wider in traditional than modern. Also, aspects in traditional are primarily by sign - by that, rule, Saturn and Neptune in conjunction presently are in square to Jupiter by sign. They are called whole sign aspects, and they are quite useful in natal work.

I find the wider orbs to be very useful, especially with Saturn - for instance, with transits to an inner planet or Fortune, I can start to feel the pressure at a 10 degree orb. Separating orbs seem to be a lot weaker.

Also, I find it useful to think of the Saturn return as a slow process rather than an event, in which the themes of the Return are weighing on you for quite a long period, long enough to be aware and think about them if you pay attention. That also means that a Saturn related transit can have effects triggered by an exact aspect by a faster moving inner planet.

Take into account that this is for natal and not mundane - a 10 degree orb in a mundane chart would not be in effect long enough for the aspect to perfect.

National egregores are not always benign. Sometimes they can even be lethal, or just somewhat dysfunctional.

Also, "nations" are not a natural thing throughout human history. The very concept of a "nation" is an artifact of European Christianity's historical development. Some historians would put its rise in Europe as late as the 1600s, as the implications of the Treaty of Westphalia (1648) slowly worked themselves out.

And it was only thanks to European colonialism that the idea of distinct "nations" was applied by military force throughout the global South and East.

Also, national egregores are not equally strong throughout the world. As I noted in a comment last week, in the West the egregores of both the United States and Germany are particularly weak ones, since each nation is a very recent formation in history. And both of those two weak national egregores were forged in large part during wars, during the presidency of Abraham Lincoln in the United States and the Kanzlerschaft of Otto von Bismark in Germany. Also, in each nation there still exist stronger regional egregores, challenging -- and even threatening -- the relatively weak power of the national egregore.

And in a time of world crisis, nations with with weak egregores are particularly dangerous places to live in, alas for both Germans and Americans.

(None of them particularly welcome hordes of newcomers from elsewhere, though small numbers of individuals who are eager and willing wholeheartedly to adopt the regional egregores might be welcome. I'm not well enough acquainted with many people from the middle of the continent to say whether any regions there have strong egregores.

As for Germany, I only spent a couple of months there one summer in the early 1960s (in Lengerich, Westfalen), so I'm not really in a position to say. (Basically, I don't think anywhere on the planet will be really, really safe over the next several decades. My own hunch is that of our more than 8 billion current inhabitants,less than 2 billion will get to live out their natural lifespans, and few if any of them will be in Europe and North America. But, as I've said before, I'm a congenial extreme pessimist, so take that with a large handful of salt.)

replies.content:
This is an excellent discussion! As Robert M. and others here have said, it's important to look at regional cultures and egregores and consider their relative strengths vis-a-vis the 'national' egregore. I must say I wonder how strong the latter is, as Robert says, when the 'nation' it represents is quite recent and / or quite diverse.

A very interesting study that can contribute to this discussion is a book called "Albion's Seed: Four British Folkways in America" by David Hackett Fischer (Oxford U. Press, 1989). The author establishes that in the early years of the US, people from 4 British regions settled into 4 different US regions: (1) from East Anglia to Massachusetts; (2) from the south of England to Virginia; (3) from North Midlands to Delaware; and (4) from the Borderlands to the "back country" of the US. He uses the same study questions about "ways of life" to analyze each original culture and how, after several centuries, these regions are still characterized by the ways of the original culture. Examples of "ways" include family life, education, attitude toward elites, work ethic, etc.

This speaks, it seems to me, to the matter of egregores moving with people, and becoming established in landscapes different from their original ones. This points out a difference between the situation in the US and that in Germany. In Germany, though political affiliations and structures have changed, in general the same people have remained in the same regions for many centuries. This supports the strength of regional, land-based egregores over national ones there.

In the US the situation is much more complex since we have regions settled by people from many different countries, overlaid onto regions where the land-egregores have long been shaped by indigenous peoples and their cultures, who themselves have long and complex histories of movements, separations, and unifications.

A question I have is: could we say that the basic *institutions* of the US, such as the Constitution and the tripartite design of the government, have their own egregore? As opposed to egregores based on populations, land, or history? Because this is what I feel, what I sense, as being the most powerful form of a US egregore. Though possibly this is a matter of wishful thinking on my part! I probably don't know as much about egregores as others here do, and I'd be interested in others' thoughts on this.

Winifred

Egregores do seem to move with people into new landscapes, but then those new landscapes also seem to change both the people and the egregores that have moved into them.

All this, IMHO, is quite important for both history and politics, but not actually taken into account by most historians and most political scientists.

I can't really speak for Germans, but (as I've posted several times before on various of our host's blogs) my father--who was in a position to know--told me back in 1960 that a huge part of the population in the USA admired Germany for its educational system, its science. its industry, and especially its eugenic policies; and (as he told me) about a third of all Americans whom he knew and worked with actively wanted to enter World War 2 on the side of Germany. (In those days England was still hugely despised on the West Coast as our nation's traditional adversary.) It was only Pearl Harbor that tipped the balance of public opinion toward entering the war at all, and entering it on the side of the Allies. He also told me that most of the Americans who had admired the Nazis didn't change their views after the War, but simply went silent about them out of a measure of shame and caution. He said I would never hear a word about this in any of my college history courses, but that I should know about it nonetheless.


My father himself had favored US neutrality in the war. All four of his grandparents had been born and raised in Denmark and he was very proud of the Danes for their quiet resistance to the Nazi occupation of their country. (Denmark has had something of an adversarial relationship Germany ever since the 19th-century wars between the two over Schleswig and Holstein.) He didn't care much for the leading Allied nations, England and France, either.

So I wasn't in the least surprised to see the massive resurgence of Nazi-style politics in Americda in the current century, once the GI generation had largely passed from the scene. It's part of our national egregore as it was shaped after our War Between the States.

The only thing I can say about modern German attitudes is that after the war there was a program of education, imposed by the Allies, to educate schoolchildren on the horrors of Naziism; and such programs generally strike schoolchildren as indoctrination, and provoke in them a quiet skepticism, if not actual resistance.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Very much so. Until stage magicians got caught up in the pseudoskeptic crusade, in fact, it was quite common for stage magicians to be occultists as well. One good example was Walter Gibson, the author who created "The Shadow" -- the first pulp detective hero with a secret identity! -- who was also a first-rate stage magician (he's the guy who introduced the "Chinese Linking Rings" trick to America) and a capable occultist, the author of several decent books on occult topics.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Gibson is a great example of this: a very smart and talented magician in both senses of the term.

Randi-style skepticism probably goes back to Harry Houdini (Erich Weiss), whose example dominated the field for decades after his death.

Houdini appears to have been a deeply self-tormented figure where occultism was concerned. He was very deeply read in serious occult literature. He seems to have wanted to find "true" magic somewhere, but despite all his efforts could only find trickery no matter where he looked. His truly enormous library is now in the Library of Congress. It includes such occult rarities as the original hand-written scrying records of Frederick Hockley (his so-called "Crystal Manuscripts"), who was a major British occultist of the 19th century.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Gibson was also involved, interestingly enough, in busting fake mediums, but he also recognized that there was real magic. His Complete Illustrated Book of The Psychic Sciences is a fine introduction to what working occultism looked like before the 1960s.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:

Houdini was also a close friend of the excellent occult novelist--and practicing occultist--Sax Rohmer, who himself was a protege of the physician R. W. Counsell, a member (IIRC) of the S.R.I.A. and also the author of Apologia Alchymiae: A Restatement of Alchemy (1925), to which book Rohmer contributed a "Preface." Rohmer also published a number of serious occult articles, including one on how to do astral projection and another on music and magic,

There certainly are folks who react allergically to magic. They fall into various categories, some with no consistent similarities between them. The most bitterly reactive category of magical intolerants that I have run into thus far would be the demon possessed/obsessed.

Some demons are tickled pink with magical practice because they can easily hijack those energies and redirect them for their own purposes. Other demons don't seem to have that particular aptitude, so healthy magical practice worries them to no end.

If someone has gotten burned by ill-considered magical practice and its resulting karma, then shielding them from magical overload would be a gracious courtesy. If, on the other hand, someone has been infested by hostile malignancy, and your magical practice leaves them feeling off, that offness would simply be your magic doing its work of purifying and protecting you. Shielding that kind of sensitive from magical work would amount to shooting yourself in the foot, as all of their ill and off feelings get redirected onto you.

I don't know of any easy way to distinguish the innocently vulnerable from the toxically parasitic, since parasites can employ a plethora of camouflaging techniques. It can be particularly difficult to awaken to the realization that ones own child or spouse or parent has been demon infested. Or, as in my own experience, that one's sibling turns out to be an irredeemable psychopath.

Since we are all of us working with the subtle energies of magic all the time (that's what willpower is, after all), I would be extraordinarily cautious before relinquishing my own focused protective practices in the hope of protecting some vulnerability-signalling other. That other may well be fully adept at protecting himself... at your expense.

— Christophe

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Where does the idea that anger is a secondary emotion come from? Books like Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving and Healing the Shame that Binds You clearly take the position that anger is what motivates you to protect yourself and others from abuse, injustice and exploitation, and those who dismiss and decry anger are often those who want you servile.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Calling anger a secondary emotion isn't dismissing and decrying it! It's precisely because anger is defensive and protective that children turn to it when they're overwhelmed by unresolved feelings of grief, shame, or fear. That can save their lives -- it certainly did with me. The point is simply that in adulthood, if that anger has gotten stuck, you can't resolve it by venting it. You have to go beneath it and process the grief, shame, or fear you couldn't handle in childhood. That frees up those emotions, and it also frees up the anger so you can apply it more appropriately in your adult life.

I got the concept of anger as a secondary emotion from my teacher John Gilbert, who learned it from his teachers. He suffered violent physical abuse as a child, btw, so knew something of the matter.

replies.title: Re: Sugar and Salt

You can certainly use sugar in magic. Recently I was re-reading Joseph Jenkins's Humanure Handbook and noticed that he mentioned Rudolph Steiner. According to Jenkins, Steiner said that humanure should never be used to grow crops for human consumption, and that in general animal manures should not be used to grow food for that species of animal. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As for what happens materially, which I think is an important guide to what might be happening on other levels given the close entrainment and symbolism of identity and self-representation across the levels, I like this characterization that I saw recently. In American Zen, one of the ways of describing enlightenment is "resolving the Great Matter", and:

https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/mZtbEKb2vYzdC2mRL/on-resolving-the-great-matter?commentId=tqR397vkeN78gEZPr

> Does the Great Matter map to the thing I'll try and point to here:
>
> > The rejection of your non-existence, bending perception and epistemics in order to make your own not-being in some sense incomprehensible, and therefore unknown/scary/something your whole system contorts itself to avoiding. Similar to other trauma-patterns which reject the reality they try and avoid, but made of the evolution-trauma of the deaths of your ancestors.

That part about "unknown/scary/something your whole system contorts itself to avoiding" is stated in such a way as to implicitly tie in with this whole other body of thinking, about how a lot of human and animal motivation is built on top of a shortcut of instinctively insisting on imaginary futures where some things go certain ways and other things definitely don't go certain other ways, and then (in a certain sense) performing one's part in those imagined futures. "One's own death" is one of the strongest holes in imagined-possibility-space a human's instincts can impose, and a hole that strong has certain recurring distortionary effects. The body of thinking is loosely organized around a concept called "predictive processing" or "active inference"; I wrote it up a little in https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/352530.html?thread=58548498#cmt58565394 .

As I understand it, the material form of enlightenment can be described as an interruption (often only made possible by long practice and habit-building) of what would otherwise be the instinctively compulsive ongoing construction of a special instinct-identifiable concept of oneself and of certain habituated illusions around that concept. This interruption has a lot of effects, both "good" and "bad", on instincts selected to manipulate the organism's behavior through manipulation of the organism's thinking about "itself". (I say "good" and "bad" in quotes, because the habits that support enlightenment often lead to the drawing-out of a sort of figurative compelling toxin from the way people normally use words like that.) One of those effects is to stop one's instincts from being able to drive so many anomalies around the concept of one's own death; and so, conversely, it's possible that the self-defendingness of those anomalies is the main reason why this kind of enlightenment is normally so much more readily achieved (pace the opposite Dzogchen strategy) by a route so slow and indirect.

(Also, enlightenment usually is somehow connected with making your "conscious self" stop suffering, but from the outside it seems like often when "enlightened" people undergo experiences that one would normally expect to require a grieving or trauma-recovery process, the rest of person still becomes distressed and impaired in the usual ways, even if the person's conscious self reports feeling fine. I'm not sure what to say about this point due to conflicting information, but one possibility is some kind of paradox about "tuning out from" some aspects of pain by "tuning into" other aspects of it.)

There's a strange parallel between, in the case of enlightenment as a phenomenon of material brains, the disruption of an illusion and the disruption of compulsive identification with an instinct-constructed concept of "self", and, in the loose consensus of the Western-esoteric worldview, the falling-away and loss of compellingness of one's material-causation-rooted thoughts and the disruption of one's compulsive identification with an incarnation-constructed concept of "self-body". However, this parallel seems to me to be tied in with the way that the supernatural tries to maintain plausible deniability or interpret-away-ability to almost all materialist observers, which leads into opening a small can of worms to try to comment about. And one of those worms in this case is a certain Zen phenomenon which is referred to figuratively as keeping a teaching device from getting "used up", which makes the whole thing fraught in such a way where I don't feel competent enough right now to go into further detail without risking spoiling something important.

Anyway, there are downsides to the material side of enlightenment, associated with the normal instinctive thinking patterns it interrupts. It's sort of a Chesterton's Fence situation, where you'd want to know what those downsides are and how to compensate for them or work around them before simply "tearing down the fence" of self-image instinct. And the work of doing that seems like an important symbolic effort for doing the corresponding work for supernatural things parallel to material enlightenment.

comments.title: The Great Conjunction, and etheric beings trapped on a desert island

For these souls, the experience of being human in this world was an unnatural prison, and this gave rise to transcendent religions, with the message that their followers belong in a world of light beyond material existence. Personally, I appreciate their contributions here, but it's also a good thing for them to get back to where they belong.

Anyway, you're probably thinking, what's my point, this has been discussed already. Yes, but this whole thing coincides with another massive astrological event -- the conjunction of Saturn & Neptune in 0 Aries, a zodiacal reset that will bring a major transformation, as it did the last time around in the days of Ancient Greece. This too is good news, as the world could use innovation right about now.

Meaning, before the present, and before the birth of philosophy and rise of Axial religions, when did this last happen? comments.title: Death cleansing
comments.content:
Hi, JMG,

I’m the one who wrote to you last week about my mom being in home hospice. She passed on Friday evening. I did take your advice and do prayers for her, and a Buddhist friend is sponsoring pujas for her. So thank you for the suggestion.

replies.title: Re: Death cleansing
replies.content:
First, please accept my condolences. That's always a difficult thing to go through.

Second, yes, alongside material cleansing, the space should be cleared and cleansed spiritually. Your Buddhist friend may know ceremonies for this. If not, burn some frankincense and call upon the deity or deities you work with to cleanse, purify and bless the space.
